Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd try to set aside time to study, but with everything going on in my life and limited resources, it rarely worked out as planned. I'm an auditory learner, so I'd often just listen to lecture recordings while doing other things around the house. Some weeks I'd barely touch my coursework, and then I'd have these intense cramming sessions when deadlines hit. It wasn't ideal, but it's how I survived.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on what else was happening. If I had work or personal stuff going on, studying just fell to the bottom of the list. Deadlines were really the only thing that forced me into action. I'd love to say I had a consistent schedule, but that would be a lie. The stress of not knowing when I'd actually get to study added to my anxiety a lot.
Q3: Usually it was panic about an upcoming deadline that got me to log in. Sometimes I'd get a notification about a new assignment or a message from a classmate in the peer support group, and that would remind me the course existed. I wish I could say I logged in out of genuine curiosity, but honestly it was mostly obligation and fear of failing. The peer support connection helped sometimes though.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. I never got to the point where logging into the LMS felt automatic or natural. There were brief periods when I was more consistent, especially when my peer support buddy would check in on me. But those moments were short-lived. The routine never stuck because something in my life would always disrupt it, and I'd fall back into avoidance mode.
Q5: The video lectures were probably the most useful for me because I learn best by listening. I could play them while doing other things, which helped when I couldn't dedicate full attention to studying. The discussion posts were sometimes helpful too because hearing other students' perspectives through peer support made me feel less alone. But honestly, I didn't use most resources as deeply as I probably should have.
Q6: I definitely ignored a lot of the reading materials and supplementary PDFs. With limited resources, I couldn't always print things out, and reading on my phone was exhausting. I also skipped a lot of the optional practice exercises because they felt overwhelming when I was already behind. If something wasn't directly tied to a grade, it usually got pushed aside.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it was directly connected to passing the course. Anything that clearly contributed to my grade got priority. I also valued things that didn't require too much mental energy because my anxiety was already draining me. If I could listen to something rather than read it, that made a huge difference in whether I'd actually engage with it.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. When you're already struggling to keep up with the required stuff, the optional content just feels like extra weight. Required activities came with deadlines and consequences, so they demanded attention even when I didn't want to give it. Optional things just faded into the background noise of everything else I was avoiding.
Q9: Yeah, definitely. I'd download lecture recordings and listen to them on my phone when I didn't have reliable internet. Sometimes I'd take notes by hand while listening and study from those later. I also talked through concepts with my peer support partner over the phone, which helped me process things. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the computer screen.
Q10: There were times when I'd be walking around, replaying lecture content in my head and trying to make sense of it. Or I'd be discussing course concepts with my peer buddy during phone calls that weren't tracked anywhere. I'd also review my handwritten notes repeatedly, which obviously doesn't show up in any system. Some of my best studying happened when I was just thinking things through while doing chores.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a video or PDF once, and then that would be my study material for days or even weeks. My internet isn't always reliable, so I learned to grab what I needed and work with it offline. The LMS would show I accessed something once, but I might have reviewed it ten times on my own. It was just more practical that way.
Q12: There were definitely periods when everything felt impossible. My anxiety would spike, and I'd just freeze up and avoid the course entirely. When personal stress hit or when I felt too far behind to catch up, logging in felt pointless. Those were the times my attendance really dropped. It wasn't that I didn't care—I just couldn't make myself face it.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was exhausted from work or dealing with personal stuff, the LMS was the first thing I'd neglect. Confusion about assignments made me avoid them rather than ask for help. Competing responsibilities always won because they had more immediate consequences. The fatigue was real—sometimes I'd log in and just stare at the screen without actually doing anything productive.
Q14: Oh, definitely. There were times I'd open videos and let them play without really absorbing anything. I'd scroll through discussion posts without actually reading them. My mind would be somewhere else entirely—worrying about money, relationships, everything. The activity logs would show I was there, but mentally I was completely checked out.
Q15: I postponed things constantly, even when I knew it would hurt me later. The anxiety made it hard to start tasks because I was afraid of doing them wrong. Sometimes the assignments felt so overwhelming that avoidance seemed easier than attempting them. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and tomorrow would become next week. It's a terrible cycle that I couldn't seem to break.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that made me engage with the LMS consistently. I'd have these bursts of frantic activity right before something was due. It wasn't healthy, but it was the only way I functioned. Without deadlines, I probably would have drifted even more than I already did. They created the pressure I needed to actually do something.
Q17: Feedback was hit or miss for me. When I got encouraging feedback, it gave me a temporary boost and made me want to try harder. But when feedback was critical, it sometimes made me want to avoid the course even more. My anxiety made it hard to handle criticism, even when it was constructive. The peer support helped me process feedback better though.
Q18: The structure felt overwhelming at times because there was so much content spread across eight online courses. Having clear modules helped a little, but I often felt lost about what to prioritize. When the structure was too rigid, I'd fall behind and feel like I couldn't catch up. More flexibility might have helped me manage better with everything else going on.
Q19: The video content being available for download made things easier given my internet situation. But the sheer amount of reading materials was hard to manage with my auditory learning style. The peer support intervention actually made engagement easier because having someone check in on me helped with accountability. The discussion requirements felt forced though, which made them harder to engage with genuinely.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me who logs in with good intentions but gets overwhelmed and can't follow through. You might spend an hour clicking through materials, trying to figure out what's due, without actually completing anything. The anxiety of being behind can make you circle around the work without ever landing on it. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity.
Q21: That actually sounds a bit like me in some ways. I might not log in often, but when I study offline with my downloaded materials and notes, I'm actually learning. Some people just don't need to be constantly connected to the platform to understand the content. If you already know how you learn best, you can be efficient without leaving much of a digital trail.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of when you're active and what you click on, but they can't measure understanding or effort. They miss all the offline studying, the conversations with peers, the mental processing that happens away from the screen. They also can't tell the difference between someone genuinely engaging and someone just going through the motions while their mind is elsewhere.
Q23: To me, being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and making an effort to understand it, not just going through the motions. It's about feeling connected to the material and the people in the course. Real engagement happens when you're mentally present, not just physically logged in. It's something I struggled with a lot, but the peer support moments felt like genuine engagement.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much my anxiety affected my ability to engage, or how the peer support intervention genuinely helped my mood even when my grades didn't reflect it. It wouldn't capture the offline studying I did, or the times I was learning through conversation rather than clicking. It also wouldn't show that I actually cared about passing—I just had a hard time showing it in conventional ways.
